claims.text: Andrew Bashiago claims that as a six-year-old he was sent back in time to Gettysburg on November 19, 1863 as part of DARPA's Project Pegasus time-travel program.
claims.rationale: Host explicitly debunks this as impossible in the verdict section: the science doesn't work, and teleporting a person would require more energy than an exploding star. Bashiago's story has changed repeatedly and follows the standard 'secret-space-program' influencer pattern.

claims.text: Nicola Tesla died alone in room 3327 of the New Yorker Hotel on January 7, 1943, and within hours government agents confiscated 80 boxes of his research.

evidence.quote:
Nicola Tesla died
alone in room 3327

claims.rationale: Tesla's January 7, 1943 death at the Hotel New Yorker is well-documented. The Office of Alien Property seizure of Tesla's papers is a matter of FBI/OAP record — approximately 80 boxes were held, reviewed by John G. Trump for MIT, and eventually mostly returned to Tesla's nephew.

claims.text: The US government confiscated Tesla's files after his 1943 death, and the FBI still has classified Tesla documents it will not release.

evidence.quote:
government did confiscate Tesla's
files after his death

claims.rationale: Host acknowledges this in the debunking section as documented. FBI Vault does contain partially-redacted Tesla files; the Office of Alien Property seizure is established historical fact.

claims.text: Tesla's radiant energy research was about wireless power transmission, not teleportation — the two concepts have nothing to do with each other.

evidence.quote:
Tesla's radiant energy was
about wireless power transmission, not
teleportation

claims.rationale: Standard history-of-science fact. Tesla's Wardenclyffe and Colorado Springs work focused on wireless power and radio, never matter teleportation. Host's verdict claim directly contradicts the Bashiago framing.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] The Bashiago narrative depends on Tesla having developed a teleportation device; Tesla's actual research has no such component.

claims.text: Teleporting a human being would require more energy than an exploding star, making human teleportation physically impossible under current physics.

evidence.quote:
teleporting a
person, that would require more energy
than an exploding star

claims.rationale: Quantum teleportation at the particle-state level has been demonstrated, but scaling to macroscopic objects runs into combinatorial energy/information problems. The 'more than an exploding star' framing is dramatic but points at the correct order-of-magnitude gap between demonstrated and fiction-scale teleportation.

dependencies.rationale: [logical] Bashiago's narrative requires human teleportation at 1968 technology levels, which physics rules out under known principles.

claims.text: Bashiago says Donald Rumsfeld ran Project Pegasus from 1969 to 1972 — but Rumsfeld was working on domestic policy in those years, nowhere near DARPA.

evidence.quote:
Bashago says Donald Rumsfeld
ran the project from 1969 to 1972, but
Rumsfeld was working on domestic policy
then, nowhere near DARPA

claims.rationale: Rumsfeld's 1969-1972 role is well-documented: Director of the Office of Economic Opportunity and Counselor to Nixon. He did not join the Pentagon until 1973 as Secretary of Defense. The host's specific factual rebuttal is correct and damning to Bashiago's account.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Bashiago's organizational chart for Project Pegasus places Rumsfeld in a role he verifiably didn't occupy in those years — a documentable error in the core narrative.

claims.text: The famously-confirmatory moment on Art Bell's radio show — when Bell corroborated Bashiago's 'ITT Nutley NJ' detail — is undermined because Bell had already mentioned his ITT Nutley job in his 1998 autobiography.

evidence.quote:
Art
mentioned working at ITT Nutley in his
1998 autobiography

claims.rationale: Art Bell's memoir 'The Art of Talk' (1998) is public record and does reference his ITT Federal work. If true, Bashiago had access to published information, which means the 'Art Bell confirmation' is not independent.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] One of Bashiago's strongest 'I could not have known this' pieces of evidence dissolves once the information is shown to be publicly available pre-broadcast.

claims.text: Bashiago claims that Barack Obama (then known as Barry Soetoro, a Columbia University student) was a fellow chrononaut on the Mars jump program.

evidence.quote: Barack Obama was a Martian cryonaut

claims.rationale: Host presents this as an extraordinary claim, then implicitly rejects it in the verdict by classifying Bashiago's entire Mars-jump narrative as follow-the-money fiction. Obama attended Columbia 1981-1983 — timeline overlap is the only element that's true. directTruth near zero.

claims.text: In 2008 Bashiago published 'The Discovery of Life on Mars' analyzing blurry NASA photos, and only years later added the 'I actually walked on Mars via the jump room' story — a narrative escalation typical of commercial disclosure figures.

evidence.quote:
he published a paper
claiming to discover life on Mars by
analyzing NASA photos

claims.rationale: Bashiago's 2008 paper 'The Discovery of Life on Mars' is a real publication. The sequencing argument (why analyze blurry photos if you've been there?) is host's valid inference about narrative accretion in UFO-disclosure communities. Matches the 'lifecycle of a disclosure grifter' pattern documented across many similar figures.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] If the most dramatic elements were added later for commercial reasons, the originating time-travel claim inherits the same credibility problem.

claims.text: Josephine Cobb's 1952 identification of the first confirmed photograph of Lincoln at Gettysburg (November 19, 1863) is an established archival fact.

evidence.quote:
In 1952, archivist Josephine Cobb was
examining Civil War glass plate
negatives at the National Archives. She
studied each plate under magnification

claims.rationale: Josephine Cobb's 1952 identification of Lincoln in Mathew Brady's Gettysburg photograph at the National Archives is a documented archival discovery. The photo is real and publicly viewable.

claims.text: The boy visible in the foreground of the Lincoln-at-Gettysburg photograph is the time-traveling six-year-old Andrew Bashiago himself, proving the Project Pegasus story.
claims.rationale: The photograph exists and shows a small figure. The claim that this figure is Bashiago depends on the entire Project Pegasus narrative being true, which host rejects. Countless photographs contain ambiguous figures that conspiracy theorists identify as time travelers.

dependencies.rationale: The 'it's me in the photo' claim only makes sense if time travel to Gettysburg actually happened.

claims.text: Secret-space-program storytellers like Bashiago share a predictable pattern: multiple program memberships, memories 'suppressed then slowly returning,' and monetization through paid speaking events, seminars, and books.

evidence.quote:
Like almost
everyone who claims they were part of a
secret space program, Bashago's story
follows a pattern

claims.rationale: Observable genre pattern across Corey Goode, Michael Salla, Randy Cramer, Michael Relfe, and others — all share the same commercial structure and narrative arc. Host's sociological claim is well-supported by the UFO-disclosure community's public record.

claims.text: The US military has experimented on children without consent through programs like MK Ultra, which is documented public record.

evidence.quote:
US military and CIA have
experimented on children. NK Ultra did
it. Multiple programs tested drugs,
psychology, and worse on kids without
consent

claims.rationale: Host includes this as part of the 'provable facts' section. MK Ultra's involvement of minors at Montreal's Allan Memorial and via sub-project contractors is documented in the 1977 Senate hearings.

claims.text: Project Pegasus claims Tesla-based teleportation machines killed 12 of 15 adult volunteers in 1968 trials, forcing the program to recruit children whose minds were more flexible.

evidence.quote:
Dr. Ragnu studied
the data from 15 failed tests, 15
volunteers, only three survivors, and
all went insane. But there was a
pattern

claims.rationale: Core element of the Bashiago narrative. No documentary, medical, or financial record of any such casualties exists. Host rejects the physics and timeline of the entire teleportation story in the verdict. directTruth near zero.

dependencies.rationale: This is the plot-mechanics justification Bashiago gives for why children like him were allegedly recruited.
